name: SLB N.V.

profile.description: SLB N.V. (formerly Schlumberger Limited) is the world's largest oilfield services company, operating through four divisions: Digital & Integration, Reservoir Performance, Well Construction, and Production Systems. The company rebranded from Schlumberger to SLB in October 2025, signaling its transition to a global technology company focused on energy innovation. Key growth vectors include the Lumi AI platform, ChampionX acquisition ($8B, closed 2025), and data center energy solutions. Founded in 1926, headquartered in Houston, Texas.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): SLB has executed a genuine strategic transformation — rebranding from Schlumberger to SLB in October 2025, acquiring ChampionX for $8B to expand production chemicals, and launching the Lumi data and AI platform for the energy sector [1][3]. The Digital & Integration division represents a new revenue stream that didn't exist five years ago, with digital revenue reaching $640M in Q1 2026 (+9% YoY) and annual recurring revenue of $1.02B (+15% YoY) [1]. AI Deployment (1/2): The Lumi AI platform provides AI-powered subsurface insights, automated drilling optimization, and predictive maintenance, with $1.5B invested in digital technology. Data center solutions grew 45% YoY with management targeting a $1B exit run-rate [1][3]. However, specific quantified AI revenue as a standalone figure has not been disclosed. Market Share (2/2): SLB is the #1 global oilfield services company by revenue ($35.7B FY2025), with leading positions in wireline, reservoir characterization, and international operations [1]. Product Pipeline (0/2): No specific named next-gen products with launch dates identified; strategy is platform-based rather than discrete product cycles. R&D Intensity (1/1): R&D spending significantly exceeds oilfield services peers, with $1.5B in digital technology investment and the largest technology portfolio in the industry [3].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): SLB's vision — 'driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' — represents genuine evolution from a traditional oilfield services identity. The rebrand from Schlumberger to SLB signals intentional strategic direction, and the 'Living Our Culture' program demonstrates effort to embed purpose into organizational behavior [3][4]. However, the vision remains aspirational rather than validated by decades of consistent execution. Community Impact (1/2): SLB publishes comprehensive sustainability reports, maintains strong MSCI AA ESG rating, and has set emissions reduction targets; the company's 'New Energy' portfolio includes carbon capture and geothermal investments aligned with broader societal benefit [5]. Employee Retention (1/2): CEO Olivier Le Peuch has led since 2019 (7 years), and the company emphasizes knowledge and technical innovation as cultural foundations. The 'Living Our Culture' program post-rebrand aims to strengthen organizational identity, but the 109,000-person global workforce in oilfield services faces inherent cyclical employment pressures [3]. Insider Buying (1/2): Le Peuch and executives maintain equity-based compensation aligned with long-term performance, but no significant net open-market insider buying has been identified.

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored as Dividend stock (yield 2.13% > 2% and 50+ years of dividend payments). D/E of 42.5% is well below the 70% target (score 1.0). ROE of 14.1% approaches the 15% target (score 0.94). Revenue growth YoY of -1.6% (FY2024-FY2025) reflects North American softness (score 0.61) [1]. Revenue 5yr CAGR of 8.6% is near the 10% target (score 0.91). Forward P/E of 16.6 is below the 20 target (score 1.0). Profit margin of 9.3% is below the 20% target (score 0.46) [1]. Payout ratio of 50.7% is above the 40% target (score 0.82, 1x weight). Consecutive years paid: 50+ (score 1.0, 2x weight). Consecutive years growth: 5 post-cut recovery (score 0.50, 1x weight). 5yr dividend growth CAGR of 5.4% approaches the 6% target (score 0.91, 2x weight). Weighted sum: 10.06/12 = Infrastructure 8.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): SLB's core business — providing physical oilfield services including drilling, completions, production equipment, and subsea systems — involves massive fleets of specialized equipment, trained field crews, and real-time physical intervention in wellbores that AI cannot substitute [1]. The regulatory environment, safety requirements, and decades of proprietary subsurface data create deep moats. AI-driven demand for data centers is increasing electricity consumption, indirectly supporting hydrocarbon production. Unlike pure services firms, SLB's Production Systems division manufactures physical equipment (subsea trees, valves, artificial lift) that requires complex engineering and manufacturing. AI Opportunity (1/1): SLB is the most digitally advanced oilfield services company, with the Lumi AI platform generating $640M in Q1 2026 digital revenue (+9% YoY) and $1.02B in annual recurring revenue (+15% YoY). Data center solutions grew 45% YoY, and management targets a $1B exit run-rate — these represent meaningful, quantified AI-adjacent revenue [1][3].

infinite_game.summary: SLB under Le Peuch has made a genuine bet on transformation — rebranding from Schlumberger, investing in digital and New Energy, and articulating a vision of 'energy innovation for a balanced planet.' This represents more existential flexibility than most oilfield services peers. However, the transformation is early-stage, and the core business remains deeply tied to hydrocarbon demand cycles. Sinek would give credit for the intentionality but note that the Just Cause hasn't been validated through multi-decade consistency.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): SLB's vision of 'driving energy innovation for a balanced planet' represents a genuine cause that connects the company's technical expertise to a larger purpose — solving the energy trilemma of security, affordability, and sustainability [4]. The rebrand from Schlumberger to SLB, the 'Living Our Culture' program, and investments in carbon capture and geothermal demonstrate strategic decisions traced to this purpose. However, the cause is still being proven — it hasn't survived a leadership transition or decade-long execution period that would earn the highest mark.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Le Peuch's 7-year tenure provides stability, and SLB's emphasis on technical knowledge and innovation as cultural foundations creates a basis for organizational trust [3]. The 'Living Our Culture' program explicitly addresses team dynamics and values alignment post-rebrand. However, oilfield services inherently involves cyclical employment pressures — SLB's 109,000-person workforce fluctuates with commodity cycles, and the ChampionX integration requires absorbing a separate corporate culture.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): SLB acknowledges Halliburton's North American completions strength and Baker Hughes' industrial technology capabilities as competitive benchmarks. The company's response to competition focuses on technology differentiation and digital leadership rather than market share warfare [1][3]. Management consistently frames competition as motivation to invest in next-generation capabilities.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The rebrand from Schlumberger to SLB, the strategic pivot toward 'energy innovation for a balanced planet,' and investments in carbon capture, geothermal, and digital platforms represent genuine flexibility — the company is actively evolving its identity beyond traditional oilfield services [3]. However, ~90% of revenue still comes from hydrocarbon-related services, so the transformation is aspirational rather than transformative at this stage.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Le Peuch committed $8B to the ChampionX acquisition to build a production chemicals franchise, invested $1.5B in digital technology, and rebranded a 100-year-old company — each representing meaningful long-term bets [1][3]. The New Energy investments in carbon capture and geothermal sacrifice near-term returns for long-term positioning. However, these decisions were made from strong cash flow positions rather than requiring genuine sacrifice.

investment_thesis: SLB is the world's largest and most technologically advanced oilfield services company, with Q1 2026 revenue of $8.72B (+3% YoY) and a $36.9-37.7B full-year revenue guide [1]. The company's digital transformation is gaining traction — digital revenue grew 9% YoY to $640M with annual recurring revenue of $1.02B (+15%), and data center solutions grew 45% YoY toward a $1B exit run-rate [1]. At Forward P/E of 16.6x with a 2.13% dividend yield and strong D/E of 42.5%, SLB offers a differentiated combination of income and digital growth in the energy services space. The ChampionX acquisition adds $838M in quarterly revenue with $199M in adjusted EBITDA, immediately accretive to earnings.
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on cyclical headwinds: FY2025 revenue of $35.7B declined 1.6% YoY as North American activity softened, EPS fell 13.8%, and Q1 2026's 3% growth was driven primarily by the ChampionX acquisition rather than organic momentum [1]. Profit margin of 9.3% is structurally low, reflecting oilfield services capital intensity. The Forward PEG of 1.92 suggests the market is pricing in modest growth, and the dividend was cut 56% in 2020 (from $2.00 to $0.875) — while it has recovered to $1.18, the payout ratio of 50.7% is above the 40% target and limits growth in a downturn. OPEC+ production decisions, the energy transition, and US shale maturation create long-term structural uncertainty. The transformation to 'energy innovation' is still early-stage, with digital revenue at ~7% of total.
key_catalysts: Data center energy solutions growing 45% YoY with management targeting $1B exit run-rate by year-end 2026, creating a secular growth vector independent of hydrocarbon drilling cycles

ChampionX integration delivering $199M+ quarterly EBITDA and cross-selling synergies across SLB's global customer base, driving margin expansion toward the 23.9% adjusted EBITDA margin achieved in Q4 2025
International revenue growth continuing to outpace North American softness, driven by Middle East national oil company investments and deepwater activity in Brazil and West Africa
key_risks: FY2025 revenue declined 1.6% YoY organically and EPS fell 13.8%; further North American activity deterioration could overwhelm international growth and digital momentum
Payout ratio of 50.7% is above the 40% target, and the 2020 dividend cut from $2.00 to $0.875 demonstrates willingness to reduce distributions during downturns

The energy transition and ESG-driven capital reallocation could structurally reduce long-term drilling activity, and digital revenue at ~7% of total is too small to offset a sustained hydrocarbon demand decline
